topic: Someone finds an unsigned birthday card in their mailbox
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mail slot rattled, a sound that always made Leo’s stomach clench. He shuffled to the door, his hands slick with nervous sweat as he pulled the thick envelope out. It was a card, plain white, completely unaddressed, bearing only a hastily scribbled "Happy Birthday." Leo stared at it, his breath catching in his throat. Who was this? He turned it over, his fingers trembling slightly as he scanned the blank front. A sudden, sharp prickle ran down his spine. He quickly shoved it into his pocket, the fabric rough against his palm. He couldn't shake the feeling that someone was watching him, that this small piece of paper held some unspoken weight he wasn't prepared to handle. He spent the rest of the afternoon pacing his small living room, unable to settle, the silence of the apartment amplifying the frantic drumming in his chest.

[story 2]

Clara picked up the card with the tips of her fingers, as if it might bite her. It landed in her palm with a surprising lightness. The ink was smudged, the handwriting looping and unfamiliar. Her gaze darted around the hallway, checking the peephole, though she knew the door was locked. A tightness coiled in her chest, making it hard to draw a full breath. *Who would send this?* The thought echoed loudly in the quiet apartment. She reread the message—just a date and a generic greeting. A wave of unease washed over her, making her want to drop the card and run. She pressed her back against the cool wall, focusing intently on the pattern of the wallpaper, trying to anchor herself against the rising tide of apprehension. Every creak in the floorboards sounded like a footstep approaching.

[story 3]

Mark found the card tucked beneath a utility bill. He picked it up, his movements jerky and overly deliberate. The paper felt strangely cold. "What is this?" he muttered to himself, his voice sounding thin in the empty room. He unfolded it, his eyes scanning the sparse text. It was so simple, yet it sent a jolt of profound disorientation through him. A rapid series of questions flashed through his mind: Was this a prank? A mistake? Someone had breached his solitude with this unsolicited item. He stood up abruptly, knocking over a stack of magazines. He walked to the window, peering out into the darkening street, his jaw clenched tight, waiting for something to happen, anything to validate the strange feeling settling deep in his core.
